concepts.title: Apply the answer to the scenario

questions.text: A well-prepared, generic answer about the topic will score full marks even if it does not mention the scenario.
questions.explanation: Across 2022-2025, generic responses that ignored the scenario repeatedly lost marks on coping, phobia and memory questions.
questions.studyTip: Name people and details from the scenario in your answer.

questions.text: Responses must apply the concept to the specific scenario and its details, not give a generic answer.
questions.explanation: Markers reward engagement with the exact stimulus, not a rehearsed general response.
questions.studyTip: Tie every point back to the scenario.

questions.text: Rephrasing the information given in the question stem demonstrates understanding and earns marks.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2024, answers that rephrased the stem (e.g. 'psychoeducation builds resilience', 'kinship supports SEWB') scored nothing for that point.
questions.studyTip: If it is in the question, it is not your answer.

questions.text: Restating the question is not an answer; you must add explanation beyond the stem.
questions.explanation: Marks come from the reasoning you add, not from echoing the prompt.
questions.studyTip: Say HOW or WHY, not just WHAT.

questions.text: When a question asks for two reasons, those reasons must always be contrasted with each other.
questions.explanation: In 2023, two reasons did not need to be congruent because the command term was not 'distinguish' or 'compare'.
questions.studyTip: The command word sets what is required.

questions.text: Reasons only need to be contrasted when the command word is 'distinguish' or 'compare'.
questions.explanation: Different command terms demand different answer structures.
questions.studyTip: Read the verb: distinguish/compare means show the difference.

questions.text: An 'evaluate' answer can score full marks by describing only the strengths of an approach.
questions.explanation: In 2024, high-scoring evaluations of CBT gave at least one strength and one limitation.
questions.studyTip: Evaluate = strengths AND limitations.

questions.text: Evaluating requires a balanced response with at least one strength and one limitation.
questions.explanation: An evaluation weighs both sides before reaching a judgement.
questions.studyTip: One plus, one minus, then a verdict.

questions.text: Longer answers with more detail and a clear conclusion always score better than shorter ones.
questions.explanation: In 2025, questions 'rewarded reasoning rather than long narrative'; top responses used reasoning, not just conclusions.
questions.studyTip: Length is not the point - reasoning is.

questions.text: Marks reward explicit reasoning (because-statements), not length or unjustified conclusions.
questions.explanation: Show the logic that links your evidence to your conclusion.
questions.studyTip: Use 'because...' and then stop.

concepts.title: Apply terms, do not just define

questions.text: Defining a key term such as 'mnemonic' is enough to show you understand it.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students who defined 'mnemonic' but did not apply it, or wrote 'memory trick', lost marks.
questions.studyTip: Define if needed, then USE the term in context.

questions.text: Technical terms must be applied to the question, not merely defined.
questions.explanation: Functional use of terminology shows genuine understanding.
questions.studyTip: Show the term doing work in your answer.

concepts.title: What is not a confounding variable

questions.text: The sample size, sampling technique or research design can be identified as confounding variables.
questions.explanation: In 2022, naming these study features as confounding variables was a common error.
questions.studyTip: A confounder is an unwanted variable that affects the DV.

questions.text: Sample size, sampling technique and research design are study features, not confounding variables.
questions.explanation: A confounding variable is an extraneous variable that systematically varies with the IV.
questions.studyTip: Confounder = an alternative explanation for the result.

questions.text: Stratified sampling reduces individual differences between the experimental groups.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students wrongly credited stratified sampling with reducing individual differences.
questions.studyTip: Representativeness is not the same as controlling differences.

questions.text: Stratified sampling improves representativeness, but does not reduce individual differences unless allocation is random or matched.
questions.explanation: Reducing participant differences requires random allocation or a matched/repeated design.
questions.studyTip: Stratify for representativeness; randomise to control differences.

questions.text: A questionnaire is a type of data collected in a study.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students gave the collection method (questionnaire) when asked for the data type.
questions.studyTip: Method = how you collect; type = qualitative/quantitative.

questions.text: A questionnaire is a data-collection method; data type refers to qualitative or quantitative data.
questions.explanation: Confusing the method with the type is a frequent slip.
questions.studyTip: Ask: how was it collected vs what kind of data is it?

questions.text: A correlational study involves manipulating the independent variable to observe its effect.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students needed to distinguish manipulating the IV (a controlled experiment) from a correlational methodology.
questions.studyTip: Manipulate = experiment; measure as-is = correlational.

questions.text: A controlled experiment manipulates the IV; a correlational study measures variables without manipulating them.
questions.explanation: Correlational designs can be more ethical when manipulation would cause harm.
questions.studyTip: No manipulation, no cause-and-effect claim.

questions.text: In a study on Alzheimer's disease, the population can simply be described as 'people with Alzheimer's disease'.
questions.explanation: In 2025, 'people with Alzheimer's disease' was judged too vague as a population.
questions.studyTip: Population = the full, specific group of interest.

questions.text: The population must be defined precisely as the whole group the researcher draws the sample from and generalises to.
questions.explanation: Be specific about who and where the population is.
questions.studyTip: Population = everyone the results should apply to.

questions.text: Debriefing should occur before a study begins, to ensure participants understand its purpose.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused debriefing with informed consent and placed it before the study.
questions.studyTip: Consent before; debrief after.

questions.text: Debriefing occurs after the study and is separate from informed consent, which occurs beforehand.
questions.explanation: Debriefing clarifies aims, addresses deception and supports participants afterwards.
questions.studyTip: Debrief = the after-study explanation.

questions.text: A line graph is appropriate for displaying categorical data.
questions.explanation: In 2024, plotting a line graph for categorical data, and using the wrong number of bars, were common errors.
questions.studyTip: Categories go on bars.

questions.text: Categorical data should be shown with a bar graph; line graphs suit continuous data.
questions.explanation: The graph type must match the nature of the data.
questions.studyTip: Categorical = bars; continuous = line.

concepts.title: Use the right statistic and the given data

questions.text: When justifying a point about variability, referring to the mean is interchangeable with referring to the standard deviation.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students used the mean (or just stated the SD) when the SD was required, and drew on data not in the given extract.
questions.studyTip: Match the statistic to the claim.

questions.text: A claim about variability must refer to the standard deviation, not the mean, and must use the data provided.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation describes spread; the mean describes the average.
questions.studyTip: Spread = SD; average = mean. Stay within the extract.

concepts.title: Name and describe nervous system subdivisions

questions.text: Writing 'SNS' is a clear way to refer to the sympathetic nervous system in an answer.
questions.explanation: In 2025, ambiguous 'SNS' (sympathetic vs somatic) and naming subdivisions without describing their function were common errors.
questions.studyTip: Write it in full and say what it does.

questions.text: Abbreviations like 'SNS' are ambiguous, so subdivisions should be named in full and their function described.
questions.explanation: Precision avoids confusion between the sympathetic and somatic nervous systems.
questions.studyTip: Name + role, not just an acronym.

questions.text: An injury to the ankle would decrease (speed up) the response time of a spinal reflex involving that limb.
questions.explanation: In 2024, suggesting an ankle injury decreases spinal reflex response time was a common error.
questions.studyTip: The reflex arc routes through the spinal cord, not the injury site.

questions.text: A spinal reflex is a rapid, unconscious response initiated by the spinal cord, and an ankle injury would not decrease its response time.
questions.explanation: The reflex bypasses the brain so the response is fast regardless of the limb injury.
questions.studyTip: Reflex arc = spinal cord = fast and automatic.

questions.text: Neuromodulators are best described simply as chemicals that are both excitatory and inhibitory.
questions.explanation: In 2023, calling neuromodulators 'both inhibitory and excitatory' was a common error; in 2025 students described one without contrasting the other.
questions.studyTip: Contrast them on method and duration of action.

questions.text: Neurotransmitters and neuromodulators differ in their method of effect and the duration or extent of their action.
questions.explanation: A distinction question needs both chemicals contrasted, not one described.
questions.studyTip: Neurotransmitter = fast, at the synapse; neuromodulator = broader, longer-acting.

concepts.title: Name a specific neurotransmitter and its role

questions.text: Defining the general role of neurotransmitters answers a question asking for a specific neurotransmitter's role in memory.
questions.explanation: In 2022, vague definitions, or not naming a neurotransmitter at all, were common errors.
questions.studyTip: Name it, then give ITS specific role.

questions.text: You must name a specific neurotransmitter (e.g. glutamate) and state its specific role in learning or memory.
questions.explanation: Glutamate (excitatory) supports long-term potentiation and learning.
questions.studyTip: Glutamate = learning/LTP; GABA = inhibitory.

questions.text: The gut-brain axis is simply the population of microorganisms that live in the gut.
questions.explanation: In 2025, describing the GBA in general when the gut microbiota was required (and vice versa) was a common error.
questions.studyTip: GBA is the link; microbiota is the microbe population.

questions.text: The gut-brain axis is the bi-directional link between the enteric and central nervous systems via the vagus nerve.
questions.explanation: The microbiota is the community of gut microorganisms, which is distinct from the axis itself.
questions.studyTip: GBA = two-way ENS-CNS link (vagus nerve).

questions.text: In the exhaustion stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome, adrenaline becomes depleted.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students wrote adrenaline rather than cortisol depletion when describing the exhaustion stage.
questions.studyTip: Exhaustion = resources/cortisol run down.

questions.text: The exhaustion stage involves depletion of cortisol and resources, leaving the body vulnerable to illness.
questions.explanation: Prolonged stress exhausts the body's resources after the resistance stage.
questions.studyTip: Alarm, resistance, then exhaustion (cortisol depleted).

questions.text: Cortisol is released only once per day, in the morning, as part of the sleep-wake cycle.
questions.explanation: In 2025, 'cortisol only released once/in the morning' and 'cortisol release is a sleep-wake cycle' were common errors.
questions.studyTip: Cortisol is a stress-response hormone.

questions.text: Cortisol is a stress hormone released in response to stressors, not a once-daily sleep-wake event.
questions.explanation: It does follow a daily rhythm, but it is released in response to stress, not as a sleep-wake cycle.
questions.studyTip: Cortisol responds to stress.

questions.text: Acute stress and chronic stress are examples of stressors.
questions.explanation: In 2025, identifying acute or chronic stress as stressors was the most common error.
questions.studyTip: Stressor = the trigger; stress = the response.

questions.text: Acute and chronic are forms of stress, whereas a stressor is the stimulus that produces the stress.
questions.explanation: The duration (acute/chronic) describes the stress response, not the cause.
questions.studyTip: The stressor causes the stress; acute/chronic describe the stress.

questions.text: Avoidance coping strategies are always maladaptive.
questions.explanation: In 2022, 'avoidance is always maladaptive' was incorrect, as avoiding a stressor is sometimes beneficial.
questions.studyTip: Effectiveness depends on the context.

questions.text: A coping strategy's effectiveness is context-specific: it must suit the unique demands of the stressor.
questions.explanation: Sometimes avoidance is appropriate; sometimes an approach strategy is better.
questions.studyTip: Context-specific effectiveness = right strategy for the demand.

questions.text: Coping flexibility means consistently using one well-practised coping strategy across situations.
questions.explanation: In 2024, where participants could not switch strategies, coping flexibility could not explain the change.
questions.studyTip: Flexibility is about switching, not sticking to one strategy.

questions.text: Coping flexibility is the ability to monitor and switch coping strategies to suit changing demands.
questions.explanation: It requires recognising when a strategy is not working and adjusting.
questions.studyTip: Flexibility = adapt and switch strategies.

questions.text: In classical conditioning the neutral stimulus and unconditioned stimulus just need to be paired multiple times, in any order.
questions.explanation: In 2022, vaguely 'pairing the NS and UCS' without stating the NS comes first was a common error.
questions.studyTip: NS must come before the UCS.

questions.text: The neutral stimulus must be presented before, and reliably predict, the unconditioned stimulus.
questions.explanation: Order and timing are essential for an association to form.
questions.studyTip: NS first, then UCS - timing matters.

questions.text: If a puff of air makes someone blink, the puff of air is the neutral stimulus.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2024, students confused the NS and UCS (e.g. mistaking the puff of air or its sound).
questions.studyTip: The air puff naturally causes blinking, so it is the UCS.

questions.text: The puff of air is the unconditioned stimulus; the neutral stimulus is the cue (e.g. a sound) that initially triggers no relevant response.
questions.explanation: The NS only produces a response after being paired with the UCS.
questions.studyTip: NS = the neutral cue; UCS = naturally triggers the reflex.

questions.text: If a behaviour is followed by relief from something unpleasant, that is positive reinforcement.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students confused praise (positive reinforcement) with relief (negative reinforcement).
questions.studyTip: Removing something bad is NEGATIVE reinforcement.

questions.text: Adding a pleasant stimulus (e.g. praise) is positive reinforcement; removing an unpleasant one (relief) is negative reinforcement.
questions.explanation: Both increase a behaviour, but one adds and one removes a stimulus.
questions.studyTip: Add good = positive; remove bad = negative.

questions.text: Attention can be described as a reinforcer without mentioning any consequence of the behaviour.
questions.explanation: In 2025, mentioning behaviour without a consequence when describing attention was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Reinforcement links behaviour to a consequence.

questions.text: Reinforcement must link a behaviour to a consequence that increases the behaviour's likelihood.
questions.explanation: Without the consequence-behaviour link, it is not reinforcement.
questions.studyTip: Behaviour, then consequence, then more of that behaviour.

questions.text: When a behaviour is learned through reinforcement, observational learning is the approach being used.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students misidentified the behaviourist approach as observational learning or classical conditioning.
questions.studyTip: Reinforcement and consequences = operant conditioning.

questions.text: Learning through reinforcement is operant (behaviourist) conditioning, not observational learning.
questions.explanation: Observational learning involves watching and imitating a model, not direct reinforcement of the learner.
questions.studyTip: Consequences = operant; watching a model = observational.

questions.text: The Atkinson-Shiffrin multi-store model of memory consists of just sensory memory and long-term memory.
questions.explanation: In 2022, omitting short-term memory from the model was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Don't skip short-term memory.

questions.text: The multi-store model has three stores: sensory memory, short-term memory and long-term memory.
questions.explanation: Information flows sensory to STM to LTM, with attention and rehearsal as controls.
questions.studyTip: Sensory then STM then LTM.

questions.text: The hippocampus is where all long-term memories are permanently stored.
questions.explanation: In 2023, high-scoring answers named the hippocampus's role in consolidating and retrieving explicit memories.
questions.studyTip: Hippocampus consolidates; storage is distributed.

questions.text: The hippocampus consolidates explicit long-term memories and helps retrieve semantic and episodic memories.
questions.explanation: Long-term storage is distributed across the neocortex, not held in the hippocampus.
questions.studyTip: Hippocampus = consolidation of explicit memories.

questions.text: The basal ganglia are responsible for forming explicit, episodic memories.
questions.explanation: In 2024, a high-scoring response linked the basal ganglia to implicit, procedural memory.
questions.studyTip: Basal ganglia handle implicit (procedural) memory.

questions.text: The basal ganglia are involved in forming and consolidating implicit (procedural) memories.
questions.explanation: Procedural 'how-to' memories rely on the basal ganglia and cerebellum, not the hippocampus.
questions.studyTip: Implicit/procedural = basal ganglia + cerebellum.

questions.text: Choosing the correct answer from a set of given options tests cued recall.
questions.explanation: In 2022, identifying a recognition task as cued recall was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Options provided = recognition.

questions.text: Selecting from provided options is recognition; cued recall uses a hint to retrieve the answer yourself.
questions.explanation: Recognition is generally easier than recall because the answer is present.
questions.studyTip: Pick from options = recognition; hint only = cued recall.

questions.text: Songlines are essentially the same as the method of loci and rely mainly on visual imagery.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2025, conflating songlines with the method of loci, or treating their processes as one, were common errors.
questions.studyTip: Songlines are multimodal and place-based.

questions.text: Songlines are multimodal (sung narrative, rhythm, movement, Country) and enhance both encoding and retrieval, relying less on visual imagery.
questions.explanation: Because they do not depend on mental imagery alone, songlines can suit a person with aphantasia.
questions.studyTip: Songlines = multimodal + on Country; aid encoding AND retrieval.

questions.text: Sleep hygiene refers to the medical treatment of sleep disorders with medication.
questions.explanation: In 2024, a question was misread because students misunderstood what 'sleep hygiene' means.
questions.studyTip: Hygiene = habits and practices.

questions.text: Sleep hygiene means the practices and habits that promote good-quality, well-timed sleep.
questions.explanation: Examples include consistent bedtimes and limiting blue light before bed.
questions.studyTip: Sleep hygiene = good sleep habits, not medication.

questions.text: Delayed sleep phase syndrome and shift work disorder are the same circadian rhythm disorder.
questions.explanation: In 2025, an option described DSPS when shift work circadian rhythm disorder was required.
questions.studyTip: They have different causes.

questions.text: DSPS is a persistent delay in the sleep-wake cycle; shift work disorder results from working against the normal cycle.
questions.explanation: Both are circadian rhythm disorders, but they arise differently.
questions.studyTip: DSPS = clock runs late; shift work = forced against the clock.

questions.text: Melatonin is released directly from the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN).
questions.explanation: In 2023, students wrongly stated that melatonin is released directly from the SCN.
questions.studyTip: The SCN regulates; the pineal gland releases.

questions.text: The SCN regulates the timing of melatonin, which is released by the pineal gland.
questions.explanation: Light detected by the eyes signals the SCN, which then controls pineal melatonin release.
questions.studyTip: SCN = body clock; pineal gland = melatonin.

questions.text: Bright light therapy works by tiring people out so they fall asleep earlier.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2024, students needed the biological mechanism; bright light therapy alone did not increase hours slept.
questions.studyTip: BLT re-times melatonin release.

questions.text: Bright light therapy shifts the timing of melatonin release to realign the circadian rhythm.
questions.explanation: Timed light exposure advances or delays the body clock.
questions.studyTip: BLT shifts the body clock via melatonin timing.

questions.text: Advising someone to eat healthily is enough to explain how nutrition improves their sleep.
questions.explanation: In 2025, generic nutritional advice without reference to zeitgebers or circadian regulation was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Link the cue to the circadian rhythm.

questions.text: Eating and drinking patterns act as zeitgebers that help entrain the circadian rhythm, and this link must be made.
questions.explanation: Zeitgebers are external cues (light, food timing, temperature) that set the body clock.
questions.studyTip: Name the zeitgeber and connect it to the body clock.

questions.text: A self-report of how tired someone feels is an objective physiological measure of sleep.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students described how to measure rather than explaining why objective measures are used.
questions.studyTip: Self-report is subjective.

questions.text: Objective measures such as EEG, EMG and EOG are used because they provide measurable physiological data, unlike subjective reports.
questions.explanation: Objective measures reduce bias and allow comparison.
questions.studyTip: EEG/EMG/EOG = objective; how you feel = subjective.

questions.text: Stating that someone is 'less aware' or 'dreaming' describes a measurable change in a sleep study.
questions.explanation: In 2025, generic statements like 'you are less aware' lacked a measurable change.
questions.studyTip: Give a quantifiable indicator.

questions.text: A measurable change must be a quantifiable physiological indicator, such as brain-wave frequency or eye movement.
questions.explanation: Measurable means it can be recorded as data, not just described in feelings.
questions.studyTip: Say what is measured and how it changes (numbers/waves).

concepts.title: Link sleep deprivation to the context

questions.text: Listing the general effects of sleep deprivation answers a question about its impact on a runner's performance.
questions.explanation: In 2025, not linking the affective or cognitive effect to the long-distance runners' performance was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Tie the effect to the task in the scenario.

questions.text: Affective, behavioural and cognitive effects of sleep deprivation must be linked to the specific performance or context asked about.
questions.explanation: Full sleep deprivation can impair functioning comparably to being affected by alcohol.
questions.studyTip: Connect the effect to the actual activity described.

concepts.title: Characteristics of a mentally healthy person

questions.text: Listing three similar characteristics of a mentally healthy person, with any description, earns full marks.
questions.explanation: In 2022, characteristics that were too similar, or descriptions not congruent with the characteristic, were common errors.
questions.studyTip: Three DIFFERENT traits, each matched by its description.

questions.text: You need three different characteristics, each with a description that is congruent with the characteristic identified.
questions.explanation: The description must clearly match the trait you named.
questions.studyTip: Match each description to its characteristic.

questions.text: On a mental wellbeing scale, a lower score always indicates higher mental wellbeing.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students inverted the scale, treating a low score as higher wellbeing.
questions.studyTip: Check the direction of the scale first.

questions.text: You must read the scale's direction correctly before interpreting, as a higher score may indicate higher wellbeing.
questions.explanation: Misreading the scale direction reverses the entire interpretation.
questions.studyTip: Confirm which way the scale runs.

questions.text: Stating that kinship and family 'support social and emotional wellbeing' explains their role.
questions.explanation: In 2024, restating that kinship supports SEWB without describing how was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Describe the mechanism, do not restate.

questions.text: You must describe how factors like kinship and family support SEWB within its holistic, multidimensional framework.
questions.explanation: SEWB connects body, mind, family, community, Country, culture and spirit.
questions.studyTip: Say HOW the connection supports wellbeing.

questions.text: Prescribing benzodiazepines is a biological factor in the development of a specific phobia.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students gave benzodiazepines (a treatment) when a biological factor in development was required.
questions.studyTip: Development = causes; treatment = interventions.

questions.text: GABA dysfunction is a biological factor in the development of a phobia; benzodiazepines are a biological treatment, not a cause.
questions.explanation: Read whether the question asks about development or about intervention.
questions.studyTip: Causes: GABA, LTP, stress. Treatments: benzodiazepines, etc.

questions.text: Classical conditioning perpetuates a specific phobia, while operant conditioning precipitates it.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students reversed this: classical conditioning precipitates a phobia, not perpetuates it.
questions.studyTip: Classical = start; operant avoidance = maintain.

questions.text: Classical conditioning precipitates (triggers) a phobia, while operant conditioning (avoidance) perpetuates it.
questions.explanation: Avoiding the feared stimulus is negatively reinforcing, which maintains the phobia.
questions.studyTip: Precipitate = classical; perpetuate = operant avoidance.

questions.text: Catastrophic thinking is what originally causes a person's specific phobia.
questions.explanation: In 2025, stating that catastrophic thinking 'causes' a phobia rather than perpetuates it was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Catastrophic thinking keeps the phobia going.

questions.text: Catastrophic thinking perpetuates, maintains or worsens a phobia rather than causing it.
questions.explanation: It is a cognitive bias that sustains anxiety once the phobia exists.
questions.studyTip: Maintains/worsens, not originates.

questions.text: Naming stigma as a social factor is enough to explain how it maintains a phobia.
questions.explanation: In 2025, naming stigma without its internal emotional effect was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Spell out the shame and reduced help-seeking.

questions.text: Stigma produces shame or embarrassment that reduces help-seeking, thereby maintaining the phobia.
questions.explanation: The chain from stigma to outcome must be made explicit.
questions.studyTip: Stigma then shame then avoid help then phobia persists.

concepts.title: Psychoeducation is an intervention, not social support

questions.text: Psychoeducation is an example of a social protective factor for mental wellbeing.
questions.explanation: In 2025, referring to psychoeducation as a social protective factor was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Psychoeducation is a treatment, not a protective factor.

questions.text: Psychoeducation is an evidence-based intervention; social support that is authentic, energising and safe is the protective factor.
questions.explanation: Psychoeducation involves challenging unrealistic thoughts and not encouraging avoidance.
questions.studyTip: Intervention = psychoeducation; protective factor = social support.

questions.text: CBT directly treats the biological, psychological and social causes of a phobia equally.
questions.explanation: In 2024, evaluations noted that CBT primarily targets the psychological aspect, not the biological or social.
questions.studyTip: CBT targets cognitions and behaviours.

questions.text: CBT mainly targets psychological (cognitive and behavioural) aspects; a limitation is that it does not directly address biological or social factors.
questions.explanation: CBT can target the cause rather than just symptoms, but its focus is psychological.
questions.studyTip: Strength: targets the cause. Limit: psychological focus only.

concepts.title: Resilience is not a coping strategy

questions.text: Resilience is a specific strategy that a person uses to cope with a stressor.
questions.explanation: In 2025, calling resilience a coping strategy, or a response to stress, was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Resilience is a capacity, not a single strategy.

questions.text: Resilience is the ability to adapt to and recover from adversity, supported by coping skills and protective factors.
questions.explanation: Supportive behaviours can increase a person's coping ability and resilience.
questions.studyTip: Resilience = capacity to adapt and bounce back.
